'language': 'ja'} [[JA:諏訪神社 (中津川市茄子川)]] {{wikidata link|Q11631918}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Suwa Shrine |画像=[[File:Suwa shrine (Nasubigawa) 2nd torii.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}Nasubigawa 1792-1 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine, former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|lt=gold/silver-hei shrine|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=silver-hei shrine|1=Kinpei-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}) |本殿= |例祭=Third Sunday of October |神事= |地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Suwa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|lt=Nasubigawa Village|Q22126369|WD=Q22126369|lt=Nasubigawa|1=Nasubigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. It is regarded as Sakamoto Shrine in the {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha entries|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} (associated) *Hayatamao-no-Mikoto (associated) === History === This shrine was founded by soliciting from {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}}. Besides this "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," {{ill|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|Q11391067|WD=Q11391067|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|1=Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu}} is also considered for Sakamoto Shrine of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. According to the history of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, Sakamoto Shrine was founded in {{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}). In {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, shrine buildings of Sakamoto Shrine burned in a field fire, and "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" is said to have relocated and enshrined at the current site. There is also a theory that Sakamoto Shrine originally stood at the site of "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū," was destroyed by fire, then moved as "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," while the rebuilt shrine at the original site became "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." When forces led by {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} invaded {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}} in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), they burned and destroyed temples and shrines in Ena District including Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, but there are no records or traditions that Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha was burned. One reason is thought to be that the war deity of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}} was Suwa Daimyōjin, and Katsuyori's maternal line was the {{ill|lt=Suwa clan|Q2975376|WD=Q2975376|1=Suwa clan}}, hereditary Ōhōri of {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}}. Until inheriting the Takeda clan, Katsuyori himself was called Suwa Katsuyori. In the early {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, there was debate whether Sakamoto Shrine was Suwa Shrine or Hachiman Shrine, but no conclusion was reached, so both shrines stopped using the name Sakamoto Shrine and adopted "Suwa Shrine" and "Hachiman Shrine" as official names. Today, as common names, Suwa Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" and Hachiman Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." === Transportation === * About 5 km from {{ill|lt=Mino-Sakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} * {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kōtsū|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|1=Kitaena Kotsu}} Nakatsugawa Park Line, get off at "Higashi-Mino Fureai Center-mae" bus stop, then 20 minutes on foot === Notes == = {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 1. Ancient shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 2. Medieval shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian period (late classical), Chapter 12 Shrines and Buddhism, Sakamoto Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すわしんしや なかつかわしなすかわ}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]] [[Category:美濃国の式内社]] [[Category:諏訪神社]][[Category:qq]]

Shrine (Q135039908)}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '岐阜県中津川市茄子川1792-1', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '岐阜県中津川市千旦林', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:諏訪神社 (中津川市茄子川)]] {{wikidata link|Q11631918}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称=Suwa Shrine |画像=[[File:Suwa shrine (Nasubigawa) 2nd torii.jpg|250px]]
Torii |所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}Nasubigawa 1792-1 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine, former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|lt=gold/silver-hei shrine|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=silver-hei shrine|1=Kinpei-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}) |本殿= |例祭=Third Sunday of October |神事= |地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Suwa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|lt=Nasubigawa Village|Q22126369|WD=Q22126369|lt=Nasubigawa|1=Nasubigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. It is regarded as Sakamoto Shrine in the {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha entries|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} (associated) *Hayatamao-no-Mikoto (associated) === History === This shrine was founded by soliciting from {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}}. Besides this "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," {{ill|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|Q11391067|WD=Q11391067|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|1=Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu}} is also considered for Sakamoto Shrine of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. According to the history of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, Sakamoto Shrine was founded in {{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}). In {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, shrine buildings of Sakamoto Shrine burned in a field fire, and "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" is said to have relocated and enshrined at the current site. There is also a theory that Sakamoto Shrine originally stood at the site of "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū," was destroyed by fire, then moved as "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," while the rebuilt shrine at the original site became "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." When forces led by {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} invaded {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}} in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), they burned and destroyed temples and shrines in Ena District including Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, but there are no records or traditions that Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha was burned. One reason is thought to be that the war deity of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}} was Suwa Daimyōjin, and Katsuyori's maternal line was the {{ill|lt=Suwa clan|Q2975376|WD=Q2975376|1=Suwa clan}}, hereditary Ōhōri of {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}}. Until inheriting the Takeda clan, Katsuyori himself was called Suwa Katsuyori. In the early {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, there was debate whether Sakamoto Shrine was Suwa Shrine or Hachiman Shrine, but no conclusion was reached, so both shrines stopped using the name Sakamoto Shrine and adopted "Suwa Shrine" and "Hachiman Shrine" as official names. Today, as common names, Suwa Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" and Hachiman Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." === Transportation === * About 5 km from {{ill|lt=Mino-Sakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} * {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kōtsū|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|1=Kitaena Kotsu}} Nakatsugawa Park Line, get off at "Higashi-Mino Fureai Center-mae" bus stop, then 20 minutes on foot === Notes == = {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 1. Ancient shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 2. Medieval shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian period (late classical), Chapter 12 Shrines and Buddhism, Sakamoto Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:諏訪神社]][[Category:qq]]

When forces led by {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} invaded {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}} in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), they burned and destroyed temples and shrines in Ena District including Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, but there are no records or traditions that Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha was burned. One reason is thought to be that the war deity of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}} was Suwa Daimyōjin, and Katsuyori's maternal line was the {{ill|lt=Suwa clan|Q2975376|WD=Q2975376|1=Suwa clan}}, hereditary Ōhōri of {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}}. Until inheriting the Takeda clan, Katsuyori himself was called Suwa Katsuyori. In the early {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, there was debate whether Sakamoto Shrine was Suwa Shrine or Hachiman Shrine, but no conclusion was reached, so both shrines stopped using the name Sakamoto Shrine and adopted "Suwa Shrine" and "Hachiman Shrine" as official names. Today, as common names, Suwa Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" and Hachiman Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." === Transportation === * About 5 km from {{ill|lt=Mino-Sakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} * {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kōtsū|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|1=Kitaena Kotsu}} Nakatsugawa Park Line, get off at "Higashi-Mino Fureai Center-mae" bus stop, then 20 minutes on foot === Notes == = {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 1. Ancient shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 2. Torii |所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}Nasubigawa 1792-1 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} |社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine, former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}, {{ill|lt=gold/silver-hei shrine|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=silver-hei shrine|1=Kinpei-sha}} |創建={{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}) |本殿= |例祭=Third Sunday of October |神事= |地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Suwa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Suwa Shrine''' (すわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrined in {{ill|lt=Nasubigawa Village|Q22126369|WD=Q22126369|lt=Nasubigawa|1=Nasubigawa}}, {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. It is regarded as Sakamoto Shrine in the {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha entries|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} (associated) *Hayatamao-no-Mikoto (associated) === History === This shrine was founded by soliciting from {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}}. Besides this "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," {{ill|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|Q11391067|WD=Q11391067|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|1=Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu}} is also considered for Sakamoto Shrine of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. According to the history of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, Sakamoto Shrine was founded in {{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}). In {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, shrine buildings of Sakamoto Shrine burned in a field fire, and "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" is said to have relocated and enshrined at the current site. There is also a theory that Sakamoto Shrine originally stood at the site of "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū," was destroyed by fire, then moved as "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," while the rebuilt shrine at the original site became "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." When forces led by {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} invaded {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}} in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), they burned and destroyed temples and shrines in Ena District including Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, but there are no records or traditions that Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha was burned. One reason is thought to be that the war deity of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}} was Suwa Daimyōjin, and Katsuyori's maternal line was the {{ill|lt=Suwa clan|Q2975376|WD=Q2975376|1=Suwa clan}}, hereditary Ōhōri of {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}}. Until inheriting the Takeda clan, Katsuyori himself was called Suwa Katsuyori. In the early {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, there was debate whether Sakamoto Shrine was Suwa Shrine or Hachiman Shrine, but no conclusion was reached, so both shrines stopped using the name Sakamoto Shrine and adopted "Suwa Shrine" and "Hachiman Shrine" as official names. Today, as common names, Suwa Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" and Hachiman Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." === Transportation === * About 5 km from {{ill|lt=Mino-Sakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} * {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kōtsū|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|1=Kitaena Kotsu}} Nakatsugawa Park Line, get off at "Higashi-Mino Fureai Center-mae" bus stop, then 20 minutes on foot === Notes == = {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 1. It is regarded as Sakamoto Shrine in the {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha entries|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Kami|1=Takeminakata}} *{{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Hachiman}} (associated) *Hayatamao-no-Mikoto (associated) === History === This shrine was founded by soliciting from {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Shinano Province|Q289250|WD=Q289250|1=Shinano Province}}. Besides this "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," {{ill|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|Q11391067|WD=Q11391067|lt=Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū|1=Sakamoto Shrine & Hachimangu}} is also considered for Sakamoto Shrine of {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}} {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. According to the history of Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, Sakamoto Shrine was founded in {{ill|lt=Taihō (Japan)|Q1346269|WD=Q1346269|lt=Taihō|1=Taihō (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=702|Q23497|WD=Q23497|1=702}}). In {{ill|lt=Enbun|Q1145071|WD=Q1145071|1=Enbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1357|Q6135|WD=Q6135|1=1357}}) of the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, shrine buildings of Sakamoto Shrine burned in a field fire, and "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" is said to have relocated and enshrined at the current site. There is also a theory that Sakamoto Shrine originally stood at the site of "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū," was destroyed by fire, then moved as "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha," while the rebuilt shrine at the original site became "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." When forces led by {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} invaded {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}} in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), they burned and destroyed temples and shrines in Ena District including Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū, but there are no records or traditions that Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha was burned. One reason is thought to be that the war deity of the {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}} was Suwa Daimyōjin, and Katsuyori's maternal line was the {{ill|lt=Suwa clan|Q2975376|WD=Q2975376|1=Suwa clan}}, hereditary Ōhōri of {{ill|lt=Suwa Taisha|Q218813|WD=Q218813|1=Suwa-taisha}}. Until inheriting the Takeda clan, Katsuyori himself was called Suwa Katsuyori. In the early {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, there was debate whether Sakamoto Shrine was Suwa Shrine or Hachiman Shrine, but no conclusion was reached, so both shrines stopped using the name Sakamoto Shrine and adopted "Suwa Shrine" and "Hachiman Shrine" as official names. Today, as common names, Suwa Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Suwa-sha" and Hachiman Shrine is called "Sakamoto Shrine Hachiman-gū." === Transportation === * About 5 km from {{ill|lt=Mino-Sakamoto Station|Q5359412|WD=Q5359412|1=Mino-Sakamoto Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} * {{ill|lt=Kitaena Kōtsū|Q11401729|WD=Q11401729|1=Kitaena Kotsu}} Nakatsugawa Park Line, get off at "Higashi-Mino Fureai Center-mae" bus stop, then 20 minutes on foot === Notes == = {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 1. Ancient shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Chapter 8 Temples/Shrines, Section 1 Shrines, 2. Medieval shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian period (late classical), Chapter 12 Shrines and Buddhism, Sakamoto Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 === External links === *{{Commonscat-inline}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Sakamoto Shrine Suwa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Suwa shrines]]
